item.title: The Math Lesson
item.author: techjourneyman@gmail.com (techjourneyman)
item.description:
The dining room table was littered with crumpled papers, a half-empty mug of lukewarm coffee, and a third-grade math worksheet that might as well have been written in ancient hieroglyphics.

Leo stared at the problem for what felt like the hundredth time: If a train leaves Station A at 4:00 PM traveling at 45 miles per hour…

“I don’t get it,” Leo muttered, dropping his pencil with a dull clatter. “It doesn’t make any sense, Dad.”

item.title: Sol-Quarium
item.author: techjourneyman@gmail.com (techjourneyman)
item.description:
The interface glowed with the soft, bioluminescent hum of a thousand stellar variables. With a gentle swipe of a three-jointed finger, the user nudged the slider. Atmospheric Hydrogen: +0.1%.

It wasn’t a game of direct control. You didn’t build cities or command armies; you simply whispered to the universe and watched the ripples.

A few millennia ago, a beautifully chaotic fluke had thrown the whole project off script. An errant cosmic ray—completely unprompted by the system—had sliced clean through the DNA of a bipedal primate. The resulting mutation made the creature absurdly, hypnotically charismatic. He started a major global religion, completely reshaping the geopolitical landscape of Orbit Three before his brief lifespan ended.

item.title: The Last Echo of Sector 7
item.author: techjourneyman@gmail.com (techjourneyman)
item.description:
The Last Echo of Sector 7

The distress beacon had been repeating for three hundred years.

In the lawless fringe of Sector 7, three-hundred-year-old signals usually meant one of two things: a hull full of degraded, useless scrap, or a lethal trap left behind by long-dead raiders. But Jax was down to his last half-liter of oxygen-scrubber fluid, and his ship, The Rustbucket, was practically running on hope and duct tape. He changed course.
